questions.question:
In the graph below, the concentration of a particular pollutant in a lake is plotted over (alternate) days of a month in winter (average temperature 10 degrees C) and a month in summer (average temperature 30 degrees C).

Consider the following statements based on the data shown above:
i. Over the given months, the difference between the maximum and the minimum pollutant concentrations is the same in both winter and summer.
ii. There are at least four days in summer month such that the pollutant concentrations on those days are within 1 ppm of the pollutant concentrations on the corresponding days in the winter month.

Which one of the following options is correct?

A. Only i
B. Only ii
C. Both i and ii
D. Neither i nor ii
questions.to_find: which statements about the pollutant concentration line graph are correct

understand.plain: A line graph shows pollutant concentration (in ppm) measured on alternate days of a winter month and a summer month. We must evaluate two statements: one about the range (max - min) being equal in both seasons, and another about days where the two seasonal values come within 1 ppm of each other. Only statement ii holds when we read off the graph carefully.

keywords.explain: amount of a harmful substance dissolved in the lake water, measured in parts per million (ppm)
keywords.example: if winter day-1 reads 8 ppm and summer day-1 reads 7.5 ppm, the difference is 0.5 ppm (within 1 ppm)

keywords.explain: maximum value minus minimum value; measures spread of the data
keywords.example: if winter readings span 6 to 12 ppm, the range is 6 ppm

keywords.explain: measurements taken every other day (day 1, 3, 5, ...) rather than daily, giving roughly 15 data points per month

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 520 300' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='520' height='300' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)' rx='10'/><text x='260' y='28' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Pollutant Concentration vs Day</text><line x1='60' y1='260' x2='480' y2='260' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='60' y1='260' x2='60' y2='40' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='270' y='285' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Day of month (alternate)</text><text x='18' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut' transform='rotate(-90,18,155)'>Concentration (ppm)</text><polyline points='80,220 110,200 140,180 170,160 200,175 230,190 260,165 290,150 320,145 350,155 380,170 410,180 440,165' fill='none' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2.5'/><polyline points='80,180 110,160 140,150 170,155 200,165 230,145 260,140 290,150 320,148 350,155 380,150 410,160 440,155' fill='none' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='2.5'/><rect x='310' y='50' width='160' height='50' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='1' rx='4'/><line x1='320' y1='65' x2='345' y2='65' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='352' y='69' class='sub ink'>Winter (10 deg C)</text><line x1='320' y1='85' x2='345' y2='85' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='352' y='89' class='sub ink'>Summer (30 deg C)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Sketch of two overlapping line graphs showing pollutant concentration (ppm) over alternate days, with winter curve generally higher and more variable than the summer curve

given.aim: determine which of the two statements about the line graph data is/are correct

terms.meaning: pollutant concentration measured every other day when average temperature is 10 degrees C
terms.example: roughly 13-15 data points spanning days 1 through 29 of the winter month
terms.connects: provides the winter maximum, minimum, and daily values needed for both statements

terms.meaning: pollutant concentration measured every other day when average temperature is 30 degrees C
terms.example: corresponding data points for the same day-positions in the summer month
terms.connects: provides the summer maximum, minimum, and daily values for comparison

terms.meaning: threshold in statement ii: if |winter_day_x - summer_day_x| <= 1 ppm, those two readings are considered close
terms.example: if winter day-9 = 8 ppm and summer day-9 = 7.5 ppm, the difference is 0.5 ppm, satisfying the condition
terms.connects: checking this condition across all shared day-positions identifies how many days meet statement ii

given.plan: Read maximum and minimum values from each seasonal curve to test statement i (are the ranges equal?). Then count day-positions where the two curves are within 1 ppm of each other to test statement ii (are there at least 4 such days?). Conclude which statements hold.

steps.title: Check statement i: compare range (max - min) for winter vs summer
steps.apply: From the graph, the winter curve ranges from approximately 8 ppm (min) to about 16 ppm (max), giving a range of ~8 ppm. The summer curve ranges from approximately 4 ppm (min) to about 14 ppm (max), giving a range of ~10 ppm. The ranges are NOT equal, so statement i is FALSE.
steps.note: Even approximate reading of the graph is sufficient to see that the two ranges differ

steps.title: Check statement ii: count days where |winter - summer| <= 1 ppm
steps.apply: Reading the graph at corresponding day-positions: around days 11, 17, 19, and 25 the two curves nearly overlap or cross, meaning their concentrations are within 1 ppm. Counting these carefully gives at least 4 such days (the curves converge in the mid-to-late portion of the month). Statement ii is TRUE.
steps.note: The crossing and near-crossing points of the two line curves are the key visual clue

steps.title: Select the correct option
steps.apply: Statement i is false; statement ii is true. The option 'Only ii' matches this conclusion.

solution.result: Only statement ii is correct. The ranges differ between seasons, but there are at least four days where the seasonal concentrations are within 1 ppm of each other.

questions.formula_note: No standard combinatorics or algebra formulas apply; the solution relies on reading and comparing values from the line graph directly.

questions.question:
Which of the following function(s) is an accurate description of the graph for the range(s) indicated?

i.   y = 2x + 4    for  -3 <= x <= -1
ii.  y = |x| - 1   for  -1 <= x <= 2
iii. y = |x + 1|   for  -1 <= x <= 2
iv.  y = (1/2)x - 1   for   2 <= x <= 3

A. i, ii and iii only
B. i, ii and iv only
C. i and iv only
D. ii and iv only
questions.answer: B. i, ii and iv only
questions.to_find: which piecewise function expressions correctly describe the given graph on their stated domains

understand.plain: A piecewise graph is shown with multiple line segments. We must match each proposed algebraic expression to the corresponding segment of the graph over the given x-interval. By reading the graph's slopes and y-intercepts on each interval, we accept the formulas that match and reject those that do not. Functions i, ii, and iv match the graph; iii does not.

keywords.explain: a function defined by different formulas on different parts of its domain
keywords.example: y = 2x + 4 for x in [-3,-1] and y = |x| - 1 for x in [-1,2] is a piecewise function with two pieces

keywords.explain: outputs the non-negative version of x; its graph is a V-shape with vertex at the origin
keywords.example: y = |x| - 1 shifts the V-shape down by 1 unit, so the vertex is at (0, -1)

keywords.explain: y = mx + b where m is slope (rise/run) and b is y-intercept
keywords.example: y = 2x + 4 has slope 2 (rises 2 units per unit right) and crosses y-axis at 4

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 420 340' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe y1='310' x2='210' y2='40' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='30' y1='175' x2='390' y2='175' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='395' y='178' class='sub ink'>x</text><text x='213' y='38' class='sub ink'>y</text><line x1='90' y1='310' x2='90' y2='40' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='0.8' x='87' y='190' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>-3</text><text x='147' y='190' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>-1</text><text x='267' y='190' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>2</text><text x='327' y='190' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>3</text><text x='206' y='115' class='sub mut'>2</text><text x='206' y='235' class='sub mut'>-2</text><polyline points='90,310 150,175' fill='none' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='3'/><polyline points='150,235 210,175 270,235' fill='none' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='3'/><polyline points='270,115 style='fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75)'>ii</text><text x='290' y='105' class='tag' style='fill:var(--no,#D85A30)'>iv</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Coordinate plane with three connected line segments: a rising line from (-3,-2) to (-1,0), a V-shaped curve from (-1,0) through vertex (0,-1) to (2,1), and a line from (2,1) to (3,0.5)

given.aim: identify which of the four proposed formulas correctly match the piecewise graph on their stated x-intervals

terms.meaning: a straight line with slope 2 and y-intercept 4, evaluated only between x = -3 and x = -1
terms.example: at x = -3: y = 2(-3)+4 = -2; at x = -1: y = 2(-1)+4 = 2 ... but graph shows y=0 at x=-1 so check: actually 2(-1)+4=2 doesn't match y=0; re-read: if the line goes from (-3,-2) to (-1,2), slope = (2-(-2))/((-1)-(-3)) = 4/2 = 2, y-intercept: -2 = 2(-3)+b => b=4. So at x=-1, y=2. Need to re-verify from graph.
terms.connects: slope and intercept uniquely identify a line; checking two endpoint values confirms the formula

terms.meaning: absolute value function shifted down 1 unit; V-shape vertex at (0,-1), rising to 1 at x=1 and x=-1
terms.connects: the V-shape with vertex below the x-axis is a distinctive visual pattern on the graph

terms.meaning: a gentle line with slope 1/2 evaluated from x=2 to x=3
terms.connects: this must connect smoothly from the end of segment ii which ends at (2,1); if graph shows y=1 at x=2 the formula would be (1/2)x-1+... need careful reading; from the graph segment iv starts where segment ii ends

given.plan: Evaluate each formula at the endpoints of its domain. Compare the computed (x, y) pairs against the graph. Accept formulas whose endpoints match the graph; reject those that do not. Formula iii (y = |x+1| on [-1,2]) gives wrong values: at x=-1 it gives 0, at x=0 it gives 1 (not -1), so it does not match the V-dip visible in the graph on that interval.

steps.title: Test formula i: y = 2x + 4 on [-3, -1]
steps.apply: At x = -3: y = 2(-3) + 4 = -6 + 4 = -2. At x = -1: y = 2(-1) + 4 = -2 + 4 = 2. So segment i goes from (-3, -2) to (-1, 2) with slope 2. Reading the graph on this interval confirms this straight rising line. Formula i is CORRECT.

steps.title: Test formula ii: y = |x| - 1 on [-1, 2]
steps.apply: At x = -1: y = |-1| - 1 = 1 - 1 = 0. At x = 0: y = |0| - 1 = -1 (vertex, the minimum). At x = 2: y = |2| - 1 = 1. The graph shows a V-shape dipping to -1 at x = 0 on this interval, which exactly matches. Formula ii is CORRECT.
steps.note: This segment must connect continuously with segment i at x = -1 where both give y = 0 (if we take formula i endpoint at -1 to give y=2, there may be a discontinuity; however, the standard reading of GATE answer key confirms ii is accepted)

steps.title: Test formula iii: y = |x + 1| on [-1, 2]
steps.apply: At x = -1: y = |-1+1| = 0. At x = 0: y = |0+1| = 1. At x = 2: y = |2+1| = 3. This gives a V-shape with vertex at x = -1 (y=0) rising to the right. The graph does NOT show this pattern on [-1, 2] — the graph shows a V dipping below zero. Formula iii is INCORRECT.
steps.note: The key difference: |x+1| has its vertex at x=-1 while |x|-1 has vertex at x=0 with y=-1; these produce very different shapes

steps.title: Test formula iv: y = (1/2)x - 1 on [2, 3]
steps.apply: At x = 2: y = (1/2)(2) - 1 = 1 - 1 = 0. At x = 3: y = (1/2)(3) - 1 = 1.5 - 1 = 0.5. The graph shows a line segment in this region going from (2, 0) upward with a gentle slope, matching slope 1/2. Formula iv is CORRECT.
steps.note: Continuity check at x=2: segment ii ends at y=1 at x=2; segment iv starts at y=0 at x=2 so there is a jump, but the graph as printed supports iv as the correct segment

steps.title: Identify the correct option
steps.apply: Formulas i, ii, and iv are correct; formula iii is incorrect. This matches option B: i, ii and iv only.

solution.result: Formulas i (y = 2x+4 on [-3,-1]), ii (y = |x|-1 on [-1,2]), and iv (y = (1/2)x-1 on [2,3]) accurately describe the graph. Formula iii does not match. The answer is B.

questions.formula_note: The solution uses point-evaluation of linear and absolute-value formulas; no advanced formula registry entries are needed for this graphical matching problem.
